description: Adds up a range or list of numbers.
example: =SUM(A1:A10) adds every number in cells A1 through A10.
commonMistakes: Including text cells in the range (SUM ignores them silently, which can hide data-entry errors).
Forgetting to update the range when new rows are added below it.

description: Calculates the mean of a range of numbers, ignoring blank cells and text.
example: =AVERAGE(B2:B20) returns the average of the values in B2:B20.
commonMistakes: Blank cells are ignored, but cells containing 0 are counted — this can skew results if you meant to exclude zeros.
Text values are ignored rather than causing an error, which can mask bad data.

description: Counts how many cells in a range contain numbers.
example: =COUNT(A1:A100) returns how many numeric entries are in that range.
commonMistakes: COUNT only counts numbers — use COUNTA for cells with any content (text included).

description: Counts non-empty cells, whether they contain numbers, text, or dates.
example: =COUNTA(A1:A100) returns how many cells in that range are not blank.
commonMistakes: A cell containing an empty string ("") from a formula still counts as non-blank.

description: Counts cells in a range that meet one condition.
example: =COUNTIF(C2:C50,"West") counts how many cells in C2:C50 equal "West".
commonMistakes: Text criteria need quotes; numbers usually don't (=COUNTIF(A1:A10,10)).
Wildcards (* and ?) work in criteria, e.g. "*west*" for partial matches.

description: Counts cells that meet multiple conditions across one or more ranges, all combined with AND logic.
example: =COUNTIFS(C2:C50,"West",D2:D50,">100") counts rows where region is West AND value is over 100.
commonMistakes: All ranges must be the same size, or you'll get a #VALUE! error.
There's no built-in OR logic — for OR conditions you typically need multiple COUNTIFS added together.

description: Adds up values in a range that meet one condition.
example: =SUMIF(C2:C50,"West",D2:D50) sums column D where column C equals "West".
commonMistakes: Forgetting sum_range defaults to the criteria range itself if omitted — usually not what you want.
Mismatched range sizes between the criteria range and sum range.

description: Adds up values that meet multiple conditions. Note the sum_range comes first here, unlike SUMIF.
example: =SUMIFS(E2:E50,C2:C50,"West",D2:D50,">100") sums E where region is West and D is over 100.
commonMistakes: Mixing up argument order with SUMIF — SUMIFS puts sum_range first.
All ranges must match in size.

description: Returns one value if a condition is true, and another if it's false.
example: =IF(A1>=60,"Pass","Fail") returns "Pass" if A1 is 60 or more, otherwise "Fail".
commonMistakes: Leaving out value_if_false returns FALSE rather than blank, which can look odd in a report.
Nesting too many IFs gets hard to read — IFS or lookup formulas are often cleaner for many conditions.

description: Checks multiple conditions in order and returns the value for the first one that's true — a cleaner alternative to nested IFs.
example: =IFS(A1>=90,"A",A1>=80,"B",A1>=70,"C",TRUE,"F") grades a score.
commonMistakes: If no condition matches and there's no catch-all TRUE case, it returns #N/A.
Only available in newer Excel versions (2016+ with Office 365, or Excel 2019+).

description: Returns TRUE only if every condition supplied is true.
example: =AND(A1>0,A1<100) checks whether A1 is between 0 and 100.
commonMistakes: AND alone just returns TRUE/FALSE — it's usually combined inside an IF to produce a useful result.

description: Returns TRUE if at least one of the supplied conditions is true.
example: =OR(A1="West",A1="East") checks if A1 is either region.
commonMistakes: Like AND, it's usually wrapped in an IF rather than used alone.

description: Reverses TRUE to FALSE and vice versa.
example: =NOT(A1="") returns TRUE if A1 is not empty.
commonMistakes: Easy to double-negate accidentally and confuse the logic — read it carefully when nesting.

description: Searches for a value in the first column of a table and returns a value from a specified column in the same row.
example: =VLOOKUP("A102",A2:D50,3,FALSE) finds "A102" in column A and returns the matching value from column C.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the fourth argument (FALSE for exact match) — Excel defaults to approximate match, which silently returns wrong results if data isn't sorted.
Can only look to the right of the lookup column — it can't return values from columns to the left.

description: Like VLOOKUP but searches across the first row of a table instead of the first column.
example: =HLOOKUP("Q3",A1:F10,5,FALSE) finds "Q3" in row 1 and returns the value from row 5 in that column.
commonMistakes: Same exact-match trap as VLOOKUP — always set the last argument to FALSE unless you specifically need approximate matching.

description: Returns the value at a given row/column position within a range — most powerful when paired with MATCH.
example: =INDEX(A2:A50,5) returns the 5th value in that range.
commonMistakes: Used alone it needs a fixed position number — pair with MATCH to find that position dynamically instead of hardcoding it.

description: Returns the position of a value within a range, rather than the value itself.
example: =MATCH("March",A1:A12,0) returns 3 if "March" is the third item in that range.
commonMistakes: match_type 0 (exact) is what most people want but isn't the default (1) — always specify it.

description: Modern replacement for VLOOKUP/HLOOKUP — searches any column and returns from any other column, in either direction, with exact match by default.
example: =XLOOKUP("A102",A2:A50,C2:C50,"Not found") returns the matching value from column C, or "Not found" if there's no match.
commonMistakes: Only available in Excel 365 / Excel 2021+ — older versions need VLOOKUP or INDEX/MATCH instead.

description: Returns a fallback value if a formula produces an error, otherwise returns the formula's normal result.
example: =IFERROR(VLOOKUP(A1,B:C,2,FALSE),"Not found") shows "Not found" instead of #N/A.
commonMistakes: Wrapping everything in IFERROR can hide real bugs you'd otherwise want to notice and fix.

description: Like IFERROR, but only catches #N/A errors specifically, letting other errors (like #DIV/0!) still show through.
example: =IFNA(VLOOKUP(A1,B:C,2,FALSE),"Not found") only replaces #N/A results.
commonMistakes: People often reach for IFERROR by habit when IFNA is actually the more precise choice for lookup formulas.

description: Joins multiple text values into one. Largely superseded by CONCAT and TEXTJOIN.
example: =CONCATENATE(A1," ",B1) joins A1 and B1 with a space between them.
commonMistakes: Microsoft recommends CONCAT or TEXTJOIN in newer versions — CONCATENATE is kept only for compatibility.

description: Joins multiple values with a chosen delimiter, and can skip blank cells automatically.
example: =TEXTJOIN(", ",TRUE,A1:A5) joins non-blank cells in A1:A5 separated by commas.
commonMistakes: Requires Excel 2019+/365 — not available in older versions.

description: Returns a specified number of characters from the start of a text string.
example: =LEFT(A1,3) returns the first 3 characters of A1.
commonMistakes: Counts characters, not words — LEFT("New York",3) returns "New", not the first word necessarily if it's shorter/longer than expected.

description: Returns a specified number of characters from the end of a text string.
example: =RIGHT(A1,4) returns the last 4 characters of A1.
commonMistakes: If the string is shorter than num_chars, it just returns the whole string rather than erroring.

description: Extracts a substring from the middle of a text string, starting at a given position.
example: =MID(A1,4,2) returns 2 characters starting at position 4.
commonMistakes: start_num must be 1 or greater — 0 causes an error.

description: Returns the number of characters in a text string, including spaces.
example: =LEN(A1) returns how many characters are in A1.
commonMistakes: Trailing/leading spaces count too — pair with TRIM if you need the length of the visible text only.

description: Removes extra spaces from text, leaving single spaces between words.
example: =TRIM("  John   Smith ") returns "John Smith".
commonMistakes: Only removes regular spaces — it won't remove non-breaking spaces sometimes pasted in from web data.

description: Replaces occurrences of specific text within a string.
example: =SUBSTITUTE(A1,"-","/") replaces all dashes with slashes.
commonMistakes: It's case-sensitive — "West" and "west" are treated as different text.

description: Returns the position of one text string within another. Case-sensitive.
example: =FIND("@",A1) returns the position of the @ symbol in A1.
commonMistakes: Returns #VALUE! if the text isn't found — wrap in IFERROR if that's a possibility.
Case-sensitive, unlike SEARCH.

description: Same as FIND but not case-sensitive, and supports wildcards.
example: =SEARCH("west",A1) finds "West" or "west" regardless of case.
commonMistakes: People confuse it with FIND — use SEARCH when case shouldn't matter.

description: Converts text to all uppercase.
example: =UPPER(A1) turns "west" into "WEST".
commonMistakes: Doesn't affect numbers or already-formatted text visually beyond the letters themselves.

description: Converts text to all lowercase.
example: =LOWER(A1) turns "WEST" into "west".
commonMistakes: Useful before comparing text values, since Excel's default comparisons are case-insensitive anyway in most formulas.

description: Capitalizes the first letter of each word in a text string.
example: =PROPER("john smith") returns "John Smith".
commonMistakes: Capitalizes after any space, so abbreviations like "USA" become "Usa" — needs manual fixing for acronyms.

description: Returns the current date, updated automatically whenever the sheet recalculates.
example: =TODAY()-A1 calculates how many days have passed since the date in A1.
commonMistakes: Because it auto-updates, it's not suitable for logging a fixed 'date created' — use a static value or a macro for that.

description: Returns the current date and time, updating on every recalculation.
example: =NOW() shows the current date and time.
commonMistakes: Same caveat as TODAY() — it changes every time the sheet recalculates, so it isn't a fixed timestamp.

description: Builds a proper date value from separate year, month, and day numbers.
example: =DATE(2026,7,12) returns July 12, 2026 as a real date value.
commonMistakes: Feeding it a two-digit year (like 26 instead of 2026) can produce unexpected results depending on settings.

description: Calculates the difference between two dates in days, months, or years, depending on the unit code ("d", "m", or "y").
example: =DATEDIF(A1,B1,"y") returns the number of full years between two dates.
commonMistakes: It's an undocumented function in Excel's formula help menu, though it works fine and is widely used — just don't expect autocomplete tooltips.

description: Extracts the year from a date value.
example: =YEAR(A1) returns 2026 if A1 contains a date in that year.
commonMistakes: Requires a real date value — text that merely looks like a date won't work correctly.

description: Extracts the month (as a number 1-12) from a date value.
example: =MONTH(A1) returns 7 if A1 is a date in July.
commonMistakes: Returns a number, not the month name — wrap with TEXT(A1,"mmmm") if you want "July" spelled out.

description: Extracts the day of the month (1-31) from a date value.
example: =DAY(A1) returns 12 if A1 is July 12.
commonMistakes: Only returns the day-of-month, not the day-of-week — use WEEKDAY for that.

description: Returns which day of the week a date falls on, as a number.
example: =WEEKDAY(A1,2) returns 1 for Monday through 7 for Sunday, with return_type 2.
commonMistakes: The default return_type (1) starts the week on Sunday=1 — easy to get an off-by-one result if you expect Monday=1.

description: Counts working days (Mon-Fri) between two dates, optionally excluding a list of holiday dates.
example: =NETWORKDAYS(A1,B1) returns the number of weekdays between the two dates, inclusive.
commonMistakes: It counts weekends as non-working automatically, but public holidays are only excluded if you supply them explicitly.

description: Returns the last day of the month that is a given number of months before or after a date.
example: =EOMONTH(A1,0) returns the last day of the month that A1 falls in.
commonMistakes: A common trick — use =EOMONTH(A1,0)+1 to get the first day of the next month, since there's no direct 'start of month' function.

description: Rounds a number to a specified number of decimal places.
example: =ROUND(3.14159,2) returns 3.14.
commonMistakes: A negative num_digits rounds to the left of the decimal point, e.g. =ROUND(1234,-2) returns 1200 — easy to overlook.

description: Always rounds a number away from zero, regardless of the following digit.
example: =ROUNDUP(3.001,0) returns 4.
commonMistakes: Rounds away from zero for negatives too, so =ROUNDUP(-3.2,0) returns -4, not -3.

description: Always rounds a number toward zero (truncates), regardless of the following digit.
example: =ROUNDDOWN(3.999,0) returns 3.
commonMistakes: Different from simply truncating with INT for negative numbers — check behavior if working with negatives.

description: Returns the absolute (positive) value of a number, stripping any negative sign.
example: =ABS(-15) returns 15.
commonMistakes: Simple function, but easy to forget when comparing differences that could be negative — e.g. checking if a variance exceeds a threshold in either direction.

description: Returns the largest value in a set of numbers.
example: =MAX(A1:A20) returns the highest number in that range.
commonMistakes: Ignores text and blank cells rather than erroring — worth double-checking data types if results look off.

description: Returns the smallest value in a set of numbers.
example: =MIN(A1:A20) returns the lowest number in that range.
commonMistakes: Like MAX, ignores text — a stray text entry won't cause an error but also won't be considered.

description: Returns the largest value in a range that meets one or more conditions.
example: =MAXIFS(D2:D50,C2:C50,"West") returns the highest value in D where C equals "West".
commonMistakes: Requires Excel 2019+/365 — older versions need an array formula workaround instead.

description: Returns the smallest value in a range that meets one or more conditions.
example: =MINIFS(D2:D50,C2:C50,"West") returns the lowest value in D where C equals "West".
commonMistakes: Same version requirement as MAXIFS — Excel 2019+/365.

description: Returns the rank of a number within a list, with 1 being the highest by default.
example: =RANK(B2,B2:B20) returns where B2 ranks among the values in that range.
commonMistakes: Ties get the same rank and the next rank number is skipped — e.g. two values tied for 1st means the next is ranked 3rd, not 2nd.

description: Returns the k-th largest value in a data set — e.g. the 2nd or 3rd highest.
example: =LARGE(A1:A20,2) returns the second-highest value in the range.
commonMistakes: Duplicate values are treated separately, so if the top two values are tied, LARGE with k=2 still returns that same tied value.

description: Returns the k-th smallest value in a data set.
example: =SMALL(A1:A20,3) returns the third-lowest value in the range.
commonMistakes: Same duplicate-handling behavior as LARGE.

description: Returns a list of unique values from a range, spilling the results into adjacent cells automatically.
example: =UNIQUE(A2:A100) lists each distinct value in that range once.
commonMistakes: Requires Excel 365/2021+ — spill formulas aren't available in older versions.
If the spill range isn't empty, you'll get a #SPILL! error.

description: Sorts a range or array and spills the sorted result into the sheet, without altering the original data.
example: =SORT(A2:B20,2,-1) sorts by the second column in descending order.
commonMistakes: Requires Excel 365/2021+.
Doesn't sort the source range itself — it produces a separate sorted output.

description: Returns only the rows of an array that meet a condition, spilling into adjacent cells.
example: =FILTER(A2:C50,B2:B50="West") returns all rows where column B equals "West".
commonMistakes: Requires Excel 365/2021+.
If nothing matches and if_empty isn't specified, it returns a #CALC! error.

description: Flips a range from rows to columns or vice versa.
example: =TRANSPOSE(A1:A5) turns a vertical list of 5 cells into a horizontal one.
commonMistakes: In older Excel this needs to be entered as an array formula (Ctrl+Shift+Enter); in 365 it spills automatically.

description: Calculates the periodic payment for a loan based on constant payments and a constant interest rate.
example: =PMT(5%/12,60,-20000) calculates the monthly payment on a $20,000 loan at 5% annual interest over 60 months.
commonMistakes: The interest rate must match the payment period — divide an annual rate by 12 for monthly payments.
pv is usually entered as negative (money going out) to get a positive payment result.

description: Calculates the future value of an investment based on periodic, constant payments and a constant interest rate.
example: =FV(5%/12,60,-200) returns the future value after 5 years of $200/month contributions at 5% annual interest.
commonMistakes: Same rate/period matching issue as PMT — mismatched periods give wildly wrong results.

description: Calculates the present value of a future stream of payments, given a constant interest rate.
example: =PV(5%/12,60,-200) returns what a series of $200 monthly payments over 5 years is worth today.
commonMistakes: Sign conventions (positive vs negative) for pmt/pv/fv trip people up — cash going out is typically negative, cash coming in positive.

description: Calculates the interest rate per period of a loan or investment.
example: =RATE(60,-400,20000) finds the monthly rate for a $20,000 loan paid off with $400/month over 60 months.
commonMistakes: Returns a per-period rate — multiply by 12 to annualize if payments are monthly.

description: Calculates how many payment periods it takes to pay off a loan or reach a savings goal.
example: =NPER(5%/12,-400,20000) returns how many months it takes to pay off a $20,000 loan at $400/month.
commonMistakes: Rate and payment frequency must match, same as the other financial functions.

description: Converts a number or date into text, displayed in a specific format.
example: =TEXT(A1,"mmmm d, yyyy") turns a date into something like "July 12, 2026".
commonMistakes: The result is text, not a number — you can't do further math on it directly without converting back with VALUE.

description: Converts a text string that looks like a number into an actual number.
example: =VALUE("123") returns the number 123, usable in calculations.
commonMistakes: Fails with a #VALUE! error if the text isn't a recognizable number or date format.

description: Returns TRUE if a value or formula results in any error type.
example: =ISERROR(A1/B1) returns TRUE if that division produces an error (like dividing by zero).
commonMistakes: Catches every error type indiscriminately — IFERROR is usually more useful since it lets you supply a fallback directly.
